name: Saag Aloo
servings: serves 2 as a main course or 4 as a side dish
intro: I make this popular vegetarian curry two ways, depending on my mood. You could blend the spinach and chillies with a little spice stock or water to make a smooth spinach purée as in the lamb saag recipe. Alternatively, you could make it as shown with blanched, roughly cut baby spinach leaves. I like to use coconut oil for this one, which is now widely available. It adds a nice, nutty flavour to the curry.

time.prep: 10 MINS, PLUS THE TIME TO PRE-COOK THE POTATOES
time.total: 10 MINS, PLUS THE TIME TO PRE-COOK THE POTATOES10 MINS

ingredients.item: coconut oil, rapeseed (canola) oil or seasoned oil
large pre-cooked stewed potatoes and 200ml (generous ½ cup) of the cooking stock plus extra if desired
baby leaf spinach, blanched for 30 seconds and roughly chopped
Salt and freshly ground black pepper, to taste

method: Heat the oil in a large wok or frying pan over a medium–high heat. When it begins to bubble, add the cumin seeds and allow to sizzle until fragrant. About 30 seconds should do.

Now add the curry leaves followed by the chopped onion, green chillies, turmeric and nigella seeds and continue frying until the onion is soft and translucent, about 5 minutes. Stir in the garlic and ginger paste.

Add the cherry tomatoes and mix it all up well. Then, add the precooked potatoes and their cooking stock and simmer for a few minutes until the sauce has thickened and the potatoes are heated through.
Stir in the chopped spinach. (If using the puréed spinach and chilli method, as detailed in the introduction, this is the time to pour it in.)

Simmer for another minute or so until you are happy with the consistency. If you prefer more sauce, add some more cooking stock or even a little base curry sauce (see quick and easy base curry sauce).
Sprinkle the garam masala over the top and season with salt and black pepper.
